student_input: problem If demand increases and supply stays constant, what happens to price?
tutor_response: Problem set. Now submit your work!

student_input: I think the price decreases because there's more demand
tutor_response:
When thinking about demand and supply, imagine you have a lemonade stand. If more friends start asking for lemonade but you don't make more lemonade (your supply remains constant), then the lemonade becomes more desirable because there's a higher demand for it than you can meet. As a result, if you decide to sell your lemonade, you might be able to charge a higher price because people are willing to pay more to get their lemonade.

In economic terms, when demand increases while supply stays the same, the price tends to rise. This is because more buyers are trying to purchase the limited amount of goods available. Think about it this way: if everyone wants a popular toy and there aren’t enough toys made, each child who gets the toy may be willing to trade away more of their other toys or save up more of their allowance to get it. The same principle applies in the market—when more people want a product than can be supplied, they often bid up the price to secure what they need.

student_input: Oh wait, I think I had it backwards. If more people want to buy something but there's only the same amount available, then stores can charge more because people will compete for it. So the price would actually increase, not decrease?
tutor_response: When we talk about demand and supply in economics, we're looking at how much of something people want to buy (demand) and how much of it is available to be sold (supply). Imagine you have a lemonade stand. The number of cups of lemonade you make each day is your supply, and the number of cups customers want to buy is the demand. If more people start wanting to buy lemonade from your stand because it's a very hot day, but you can't make any more lemonade than usual because you only have so many lemons and sugar, the demand for lemonade has increased but your supply remains the same. When there's more demand for a product but the supply doesn't increase, people are willing to pay more to get their hands on it. So, you might decide to raise the price of your lemonade to reflect its higher value to customers. This scenario illustrates a fundamental principle in economics: when demand goes up and supply stays the same, prices tend to rise. This is because there are more buyers competing for the limited amount of goods, driving the price upwards. Conversely, if more lemonade could be made (increasing supply), the price might not go up as much because there would still be enough lemonade to satisfy everyone who wants it.

student_input: Wait, I think I mixed something up in my Black Friday example. If the store is advertising TVs at a lower price, wouldn't that be the opposite of what we were talking about? I said demand goes up and supply is limited, but then the price is actually lower than normal, not higher.
tutor_response:
When we talk about how increased demand affects prices in situations where supply is limited, we're delving into one of the fundamental concepts in economics: supply and demand. Imagine you have a lemonade stand, and you make only ten glasses of lemonade each day because that's all the lemons you can squeeze. On a hot summer day, many thirsty people want your lemonade, so there's high demand. But you still only have those ten glasses available. When there's more demand than supply, the price of your lemonade tends to go up because people are willing to pay more to get it. They know it’s something they really want but can’t find elsewhere easily.

To connect this to everyday experiences, think about concert tickets or popular toys during the holiday season. If a band is only playing a few shows, and lots of fans want to see them, ticket prices might skyrocket because the venue can't accommodate everyone who wants to attend. Similarly, if a new toy becomes very popular, stores might not be able to keep up with the number of people wanting to buy it, leading to higher prices as eager parents compete to get their hands on the toy for their children.

In summary, when demand increases and supply remains limited, prices tend to rise because consumers are willing to pay more to satisfy their needs or desires for the scarce product. Advertised low prices during Black Friday are typically meant to attract customers, but once the initial stock is sold out, the remaining items may indeed experience higher prices due to the increased competition among buyers.
